Humanity has given way to robotic androids and most time is spent inside of the metaverse. It seems like Meta travel and Meta days are better than real life for everyone....but one. Yoshimi longs for the thrill of a hike, the touch of wild animals and the thrill of companionship and she's ready to spread the word. Despite the dystopian premise, this raunchy laugh out loud comedy delivers!

Title: Chaise Longue
Comment: Estranged aunt dies and leaves a fortune to a normal woman in her 30s. She tries to fit in with high society but quickly learns there are layers of wealthy people and, despite the new money she throws at it, is constantly treated as second class. The opening credits show her trying to do rich person things, each ending in a freeze frame of failure (being given a glass of champagne but then getting bumped and dropping it, getting a steak from Salt Bea but he gets salt on her). Hard to be rich.

Comment: This was a song about the lead singers battle with an eating disorder and I always thought was beautifully written. It really gets across the desperation he felt and how even though he knew it was killing him he still felt he needed it. The "sandpaper years corode the film" reference to the effect vomiting so often had on his throat I always found to be such a poetic way to phrase something frankly grotesque.

Title: Sunday Mornin' Comin' Down
Comment:
“All those words in the dictionary but no one else before Kris knew what damn order they came in.” - Larry Gatlin

An example all Nashville writers should mimic. You’re placed in the South even though he doesn’t say it directly. Allusions to church/religion while still authentic and relatable. Common country music loneliness theme and alcohol references without feeling contrived. All from a hippie Rhodes scholar from California.

Title: The Ballad Of Peter Pumpkinhead (feat. Ellen Reid)
Album: Playlist: The Very Best Of Crash Test Dummies
Comment: Magpie enters to a very quirky and distinctive intro. Magpie is an oddball that aligns with no one, and although fans love him they all agree something ain’t right. His appearances are random but highly consequential as he tends to disrupt others’ matches. Before he enters, announcers often say things like “this one is all but over. The only thing that’d save him now is if that wacko Magpie showed up and I don’t see tha-***guitar riff, drums, ‘Let’s Begin’***wah?! No! It can’t be!”

Title: Ali Ali
Comment:
A champion of the Curry Clash Smackdown, The Maharajah of Mayhem has come all the way from India to make butter chicken out of the jabronis in America. 

Ali Ali Ali begins…

He enters listening from one side of the stadium and hears the retort of the song from the other side. 

He runs down the aisle and enters the ring. 

Suddenly from above - a group of Indian dancers lowers and they have a traditional Indian dance off. At the end everyone yells Talley Ho in unison. 

The crowd goes wild.

Title: Brass Monkey
Comment: Johnny Swagg is an arrogant dick. He clearly wants to be the next Scott Hall but he comes across as more of a douche and less of a bad boy. To the great chagrin of fans everywhere he finds himself on the winning side often and has made his way to the elite ranks, but hardly on his own merits and instead by riding the coattails of the truly great bad boys.

Title: The Birdwatcher
Comment: As Vulfman enters the stadium the crowds reaction is mixed, never knowing what to expect from this enigmatic wrestler who seems equally content flying south nosediving in for the kill. Is this song fear inducing, of course not, but this little earworm will be stuck in your head like the one he caught at 5 am, meticulously honing his craft. As he flutters through the ring taking strategic flights, issuing melodic taunts, the fans will demand the sports great signature move, the Vulf Peck.

Title: Cocaine Jesus
Comment: Jesus, hellfire and brimstone, and as it turns out enough cocaine to kill Keith Richards. Disgraced 700 club televangelist "Brimstone" Bob Hagerty famous mission trip to Juarez Mexico ended with the St. Lucia Children's Home missing $250k of donations and Brimstone thundering down the 110 in a $150k Mercedes with a $1,500 an hour escort and a kilo of cocaine. But, heaven's loss is the WWE's gain as live tonight he faces Ric Flare in a walkers and canes showdown!

Title: Istanbul (Not Constantinople)
Comment:
It's literally the ear-worm of a lifetime. This jingle is more penetrating than the TitanUp theme or the McDonalds jingle.

Growing up being driven by my father to school also meant being subjected to his music. We had [at least] a straight year of nothing but TMBG. This song likely contributed to Turkey being one of the first countries I traveled to as an adult, which I HIGHLY RECOMMEND; eat the Manti, it's delicious. Honestly, I would share more, but it's nobody's business by the Turks.
Round Name: In the wink of a young girls eye

Title: Reach Out I'll Be There
Comment: I really can't remember very much from my childhood, but anything that I do is accompanied by music. My mom's love of Motown was such a departure from the pop music on the radio and the classic rock everywhere else. I think we probably only had a few four tops and greatest hits cassettes but I can still remember popping a cassette into the console driving around in the back seat. Without a doubt, this was the coolest song I had ever heard at the time and it felt like it was breaking the rules.

Title: Apostrophe'
Comment:
Think Boogie Nights vibes set on Venice Beach. The camera fades in to a close-up of skates being laced up. Shots of bodybuilders working out, kites flying, ice cream scoops dripping, lotion being applied, volleyballs being spiked, waves crashing, and our skater beginning his skate down the boardwalk until the music fades slightly at 1:18. At exactly 1:19 we see a close up of a slick trade-off -- money trading place with a dime bag of coke. Our skater spins away and continues a fluid almost choreographed series of transactions. The boardwalk is his corner.

This slick drug trade montage continues until the crash symbol hits at exactly 3:27 as the camera cuts to a bar at the end of Venice Beach and close-up of an ice cold coke bottle being cracked open. Who says business can't be stylish?

Title: Asleep at the Wheel
Artist(s): Working for a Nuclear Free City
Comment:
On a cliff that looked like it was deeply tired of supporting buildings, Tilda Thatch stirred from a nap she hadn’t meant to take. She was an inventor by trade, an outlaw by accident, and currently the only person mildly inconvenienced by Lord Vexmoor’s joy-sucking machine. He had stolen her design, mispronounced her name in a press conference, and now expected no retaliation. That was his first mistake.

As the music kicked in with far too much optimism, Tilda and her assistant Pip scrambled to assemble something vaguely heroic out of springs, old soup tins, and a suspiciously sentient toaster. Nothing made sense, several things caught fire, and at one point the floor disappeared for five seconds. But by the time the chorus hit, they had something that beeped, glowed, and made Pip cry a little. It would have to do.

Anticipation builds the closer the two groups get.

Title: Chicago Summer
Comment: This was supposed to have a deep, poetic backstory... but I didn’t have time to write it. I was too busy soaking in geothermal pools, chasing waterfalls, and eating fermented shark in Iceland. Every time I tried to sit down and type something meaningful, a glacier or puffin distracted me. So instead of a story, you get this: a vibe captured in a song, born from the heat of a Chicago summer but released with the chill of Icelandic winds still in my hair.
Round Name: Rising up to the challenge of our rivals

Title: SPACE
Comment: I was going to have an epic origin story involving stars, solitude, and the infinite cosmos of the human heart—but I didn’t write it. I was in Iceland, standing on lava fields, watching waterfalls ripple across the horizon like the track’s bassline. I told myself I’d get to it after the next hot spring or maybe after the next bowl of lamb stew… but I didn’t. So now, all I’ve got is this: the song’s called "Space"—and I took some.
